edge: Images captured in low-light conditions often suffer from significant quality degradation.
Recent works have built a large variety of deep Retinex-based networks to enhance low-light images.
The Retinex-based methods require decomposing the image into reflectance and illumination components.
Decomposing the image into reflectance and illumination components is a highly ill-posed problem.
Previous works addressed the ill-posed problem of decomposing images into reflectance and illumination components.
Previous works addressed the problem by imposing some additional priors or regularizers.
Finding an effective prior or regularizer that can be applied in various scenes is challenging.
The performance of the model suffers from too many additional constraints.
We propose a contrastive learning method for Retinex decomposition.
Our method allows training our Retinex-based model without elaborate hand-crafted regularization functions.
Rather than estimating reflectance and illuminance images and representing the final images as their element-wise products.
Our regularizer-free Retinex decomposition and synthesis network extracts reflectance and illuminance features and synthesizes them end-to-end.
Extensive experimental results demonstrate that our proposed methods can achieve superior performance.
Low-light images often suffer from visual degradations such as poor visibility and low contrast.
The darker the area in an image, the more the information is lost, making it harder to recover the image with good quality.
Computer vision systems may malfunction or fail completely in low-light conditions.
If dark regions can be removed or alleviated after low-light image enhancement, images can become more clear.
Low-light image enhancement can potentially benefit many computer vision applications.
Traditional methods for low-light image enhancement can be divided into two categories, one based on histogram equalization and the other based on Retinex theory.
Deep learning based approaches for low-light image enhancement have received extensive attention recently, replacing handcrafted features with new methods.
Image enhancement methods like deep Retinex-based methods use deep neural networks to automatically learn features for enhancing low-light images.
Deep Retinex-based methods build on the Retinex theory which models color perception in human vision on natural scenes.
Deep Retinex-based methods deal with the highly ill-posed problem of Retinex decomposition.
Retinex decomposition poses challenging issues due to the absence of well-defined ground truth for reflectance and illumination in real images.
To address the challenging issues in Retinex decomposition, a contrastive learning method is proposed for improved performance.
A self-knowledge distillation method is used in Retinex decomposition to enhance the synthesis process for improved results.

node_2: regularizer-free retinex decomposition and synthesis network (rfr)
edge: The proposed method of regularizer-free Retinex decomposition and synthesis network (RFR) aims to extract reflectance and illumination features for image enhancement.
Reflectance and illumination are the components into which an image can be decomposed according to the Retinex theory.
The loss function named Weighted Normalized Temperature-Scaled Cross-Entropy Loss is proposed for contrastive learning.
A progressive learning strategy is proposed for self-knowledge distillation to enhance network training.
Retinex-based methods require decomposing images into reflectance and illumination components as per Retinex theory.

The context mentions that some approaches used additional priors or regularizers to train decomposition networks. However, it is difficult to find effective priors or regularizers that can be applied in various scenes. Inaccurate priors or regularizers may lead to artifacts and color deviations. This relationship indicates that the use of additional priors or regularizers can impact the Retinex decomposition process.

The context describes the objective function of Retinex decomposition, which includes terms like reflectance, illuminance, Frobenius norm, and regularizers. This relationship shows that the objective function is a crucial aspect of the Retinex decomposition process.

The text mentions that joint optimization with too many additional constraints may lead to possible suboptimal solutions. This indicates that the relationship between joint optimization and suboptimal solutions is based on the challenge of balancing mismatched goals.

The context highlights the need for introducing tricks during model training, such as adding pure black or pure white images to the training data. This relationship suggests that the use of additional tricks during model training is necessary for better results in the Retinex decomposition process.

The text mentions using only the most fundamental assumptions in the Retinex theory as the objective function for Retinex decomposition. This relationship signifies the importance of fundamental assumptions in guiding the decomposition process.
